name: Ebisu
description: Ebisu is one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin) in Japanese folklore and religion. He is primarily regarded as the god of fishermen, prosperity, and commerce, and is often depicted with a jovial demeanor. Unlike most of the other Shichi Fukujin, Ebisu is considered to be of purely Japanese origin, not derived from Buddhist or Taoist influences. His origins are rooted in the hiruko myth, where he was born deformed and cast out to sea. Eventually, he was rescued and enshrined, becoming the benevolent deity known today. Ebisu is frequently represented wearing a tall hat, holding a fishing rod in one hand and a red sea bream (tai) in the other, symbolizing good fortune and a bountiful catch. He is a popular figure in Japanese culture, and his image can be found in shops, restaurants, and homes as a symbol of prosperity and good luck. His worship is particularly strong among merchants, fishermen, and those seeking success in business ventures. While initially an outcast figure, Ebisu's transformation into a beloved deity highlights themes of resilience and redemption within Japanese mythology.
subtitle: God of Fishermen and Prosperity
titles: The Lucky God of the Sea

keyMyths.title: The Hiruko Myth
keyMyths.description: Ebisu's origins are traced back to the myth of Hiruko, the first child of Izanagi and Izanami. Born deformed and without bones, Hiruko was deemed unfit to be a god and was cast adrift in a reed boat. This myth explains Ebisu's initial association with marginality and his later transformation into a benevolent deity. The story emphasizes themes of imperfection and the potential for redemption and acceptance within the divine realm. Later accounts suggest Hiruko eventually washed ashore and was cared for, eventually becoming the Ebisu we know today.
keyMyths.source: Aston, W. G. (1896). *Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697*.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu and the Sea Bream
keyMyths.description: A common depiction of Ebisu involves him holding a red sea bream (tai). This imagery is associated with good fortune and a plentiful catch for fishermen. The sea bream is considered a delicacy and a symbol of celebration in Japan, further emphasizing Ebisu's role as a bringer of prosperity and abundance. The myth reinforces Ebisu's connection to the sea and his benevolent protection over those who depend on its resources for their livelihood. This image is ubiquitous in commercial settings.
keyMyths.source: Hearn, Lafcadio. (1905). *Japan: An Attempt at Interpretation*.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu in the Seven Lucky Gods
keyMyths.description: Ebisu is one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin), a group of deities representing various aspects of good fortune and happiness. Together, they are often depicted traveling on a treasure ship (Takarabune), bringing blessings to those who venerate them. Ebisu's inclusion in this group solidifies his status as a prominent figure in Japanese popular religion and reinforces his association with prosperity, commerce, and the overall well-being of individuals and communities. The Takarabune is a common image associated with the New Year.
keyMyths.source: Roberts, Jeremy. (2009). *Japanese Mythology A to Z*.
